text: Huangbo Xiyun, the ninth-century Chan master, warned his students against what he called 'the sickness of conceptual elaboration' — the mind's tendency to build a story around every experience. A sensation of cold is noted; immediately the mind appends past winters, self-pity, plans for warmth. Observation has become narration. The instruction in Chan, as in vipassana, is to stop one thought-length earlier: see the cold, do not become the memoir. Psychologist Ethan Kross at Michigan calls this habit 'chatter' — the self-referential loop that transforms experience into self-story. His research shows that brief linguistic distance — referring to oneself by name rather than 'I' — interrupts the loop with measurable speed.
question: According to the passage, what does Huangbo Xiyun call the mind's tendency to build a story around every experience?
